coredata.dc:title: Eu:Y2O3 highly dispersed fluorescent PVA film as turn off luminescent probe for enzyme free detection of H2O2
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Dhananjay
Umrao, Sima
Mishra, Himanshu
Srivastava, Rohit Ranjan
Srivastava, Monika
Srivastava, Anchal
Srivastava, S.K.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel sensing scaffold consisting highly dispersed Eu:Y2O3 fluorescent flexible film of poly vinyl alcohol (EYCP) was synthesized by simple mixing method and applied to facilitate non-enzymatic detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) by turn off probe of fluorescence. The fluorescence spectra of EYCP consisting number of emission sharp transitions from excited, 5D0→7Fj (j =0, 1, 2, 3, 4) energy levels of doped Eu3+ ion in Y2O3 host. In the presence of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of the EYCP film was quenched due to the reduction of electron–hole pair recombination in Eu centers by electron transfer from Eu–O excited state to H2O2 energy level rather than 5D0 state of Eu3+, which reduces the number of electron in 5D0 state. The EYCP film shows excellent fluorescence quenching in presence of H2O2 by significantly increasing concentration of H2O2 and completely quenched at ∼150μM. A linear relationship is observed between 0.0 and 60μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.989. H2O2 sensing is also compared with the EYC nanoparticles. This study is expected to have a significant impact on further study of the Eu:Y2O3 fluorescent flexible film for wide range applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Poly vinyl alcohol
Fluorescence
Non-enzymatic
H2O2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of europium ion doped yttrium oxide (Eu:Y2O3) complex
Preparation of fluorescent film of Eu:Y2O3–poly vinyl alcohol (PVA)
Mechanism for fluorescence quenching based H2O2 detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of the synthesis of fluorescent Eu3+ ion doped Y2O3 polymer film.
(a) TEM image of EYC NPs. (b) HRTEM of EYC NPs. (c) SAED pattern of EYC NPs (d) and (e) TEM images of EYC NPs decorated in PVA. (f) SAED patterned of EYCP.
(a) X-ray Diffraction pattern and (b) RAMAN spectra of Eu:Y2O3 and Eu:Y2O3 dispersed PVA film.
(a) FTIR spectrum of Eu:Y2O3 and Eu:Y2O3 decorated PVA film and (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of PVA, Eu:Y2O3 and Eu:Y2O3 decorated PVA film.
(a) PL excitation spectra and (b) PL emission spectra of Eu:Y2O3.
Energy level diagram of excitation and emission from Eu3+ ions incorporated in Y2O3 host.

(a) Fluorescence of EYC in the presence of different concentration of H2O2 (0 to 150μM) and (b) fluorescence of EYCP film in the presence of different concentration of H2O2 (0 to 150μM). (c) Optical image of EYCP film under normal light (i), under UV light without H2O2 (ii), and in presence of 150μM H2O2 (iii).

(a) Stern–Volmer plot vs. H2O2 concentration and linear fit within the linear range of dynamic quenching shown in inset of figure for EYC NPs, and (b) Lineweaver–Burk plot of static mechanism, and (c) Stern–Volmer plot vs. H2O2 concentration and linear fit within the linear range of dynamic quenching shown in inset of figure for EYCP film, and (d) Lineweaver–Burk plot of static mechanism.
Energy level diagram showing quenching effect on emission of Eu ion incorporated on Y2O3 host in presence of H2O2.
Comparative table of analytical parameters and Stern–Volmer quenching constant K sv, where DL – Detection limit; LR – linear range of calibrations curve, K sv – Stern–Volmer constant.

head.ce:title: Eu:Y2O3 highly dispersed fluorescent PVA film as turn off luminescent probe for enzyme free detection of H2O2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics (MMV), Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi, 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics (MMV), Banaras Hindu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi, 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Technology, IIT (BHU), Varanasi,221005, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel sensing scaffold consisting highly dispersed Eu:Y2O3 fluorescent flexible film of poly vinyl alcohol (EYCP) was synthesized by simple mixing method and applied to facilitate non-enzymatic detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) by turn off probe of fluorescence. The fluorescence spectra of EYCP consisting number of emission sharp transitions from excited, 5D0→7Fj (j =0, 1, 2, 3, 4) energy levels of doped Eu3+ ion in Y2O3 host. In the presence of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of the EYCP film was quenched due to the reduction of electron–hole pair recombination in Eu centers by electron transfer from Eu–O excited state to H2O2 energy level rather than 5D0 state of Eu3+, which reduces the number of electron in 5D0 state. The EYCP film shows excellent fluorescence quenching in presence of H2O2 by significantly increasing concentration of H2O2 and completely quenched at ∼150μM. A linear relationship is observed between 0.0 and 60μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.989. H2O2 sensing is also compared with the EYC nanoparticles. This study is expected to have a significant impact on further study of the Eu:Y2O3 fluorescent flexible film for wide range applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent few years, the sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has shown its importance because of its wide use in various field including food, biological analysis, and environment as well as its aberrant production under the serious pathological conditions such as cancer cells, ageing, diabetes and neurodegenerative diseases [1–8]. For the detection of H2O2, several sensitive methods have been developed based on enzyme (such as horseradish peroxidase) [3] and enzyme-free (like graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) based detection) [2,9]. However, the enzymes can critically degrade the durability and life of biosensors because of their highly reactivity with surroundings resulting changes in its physical and chemical conditions [10]. More importantly, they are easily denatured by even a small increase in temperature and are highly susceptible to presence of other chemical substances and PH. Therefore, the enzyme-free detection of H2O2 and other biomolecules has received much a significant interest for reliable and repeatable biosensors.

However, to date, many different approaches have been developed for detection of H2O2 including different electrochemical methods, spectrophotometry, UV–vis spectroscopy, chromatography, as well as fluorescence [1,2,9]. However, fluorescence-based method has paid much attention due to special advantages for biological analysis such as fluorescence is extremely sensitive and can be measured by varying different parameter and damage of host system is little or none after fluorescence measurements [11–13].

Among these fluorescence-based H2O2 sensors, traditional organic fluorophores (e.g. organic dyes) are widely used as recognition elements. Recently, fluorescent semiconductor or quantum dots (QDs) have also attracted much attention due to their broad absorption with narrow emission with high brightness and good stability [14–16]. However, the synthesis of QDs is little difficult with multiple step and need some specific equipment. Therefore, there is need of some other fluorescent nanomaterials which can be utilized for fluorescent sensing purpose with enhanced performance. Now days, rare earth ions doped luminescent nanomaterials are highlighted as fluorescent materials with high yield which have been used in various optoelectronic devices including solar cell, phosphor, color display etc. [17–20].

Among the rare earth materials, Y2O3 is most important host for lanthanides doped phosphors like europium doped phosphors (Eu:Y2O3) [8,10]. Y2O3 provides suitable sites where (Eu3+) can be easily substituted by other trivalent rare earth ions without additional charge compensation. Various work have been reported which show strong luminescence of Eu3+:Y2O3 nanostructures and used in different applications such as light emitting diodes, sensing etc. [21–23]. Y2O3:Eu nanocrystal used as a phosphor for white light emitting diodes [24]. Eshagh group used Eu+3:Y2O3 as turn off luminescence probes for detection of Pyridoxine [25]. Additionally, polymers provide an avenue for loading and stabilizing nanostructures as the supporting materials. Sayantani et al. [26] synthesized pure rare earth nanoparticles in biocompatible polymer matrices. Poly vinyl alcohol (PVA) has many attractive features like its film is flexible, transparent and can be formed easily. PVA does not affect the fluorescence properties of rare earth metal doped oxide. Therefore, we have taken PVA for flexible and transparent film formation for colorimetric detection of hydrogen peroxide.

In this study, Eu:Y2O3 doped fluorescent film of PVA was synthesized successfully by simple mixing method and used for detection of H2O2. The Eu:Y2O3 (EYC) exhibit strong and narrow spectrum in red color range. The fluorescence of EYC-PVA film (EYCP film) quenched after addition of hydrogen peroxide. This colorimetric sensitivity of EYCP film towards H2O2 increases significantly. We have also compared the H2O2sensing with the EYC nanoparticles and found that the fluorescence quenching is similar to the EYCP film.

Europium oxide (Eu2O3), yttrium oxide (Y2O3), poly vinyl alcohol (PVA), nitric acid (HNO3) and urea (NH2CONH2), were purchased from Merck and Molychem and have been used without any further treatment or purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of europium ion doped yttrium oxide (Eu:Y2O3) complex
ce:section.ce:para: Eu:Y2O3 fluorescent complex (EYC) have been synthesized using a solution combustion method Briefly, Eu2O3 (0.017g) was added to a solution of HNO3 (2ml) and deionized water (10ml) and make a transparent solution by simple shaking. A transparent solution of Y2O3 was also made by dissolving 1.12g of Y2O3 in mixed solution of HNO3 (2ml) and deionized water (20ml). On other hand, a transparent solution of urea was made by adding 2g of NH2CONH2 into 10ml of deionized water. After making all three solutions separately, mix them each other in a quartz beaker and put on normal home heater (1000W). The free water evaporated and combustion takes place within 5min. Finally, milky white powder of fluorescent EYC have been found and further used for measurements and film formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent film of Eu:Y2O3–poly vinyl alcohol (PVA)
ce:section.ce:para: To make fluorescent film of Eu:Y2O3–PVA (EYCP film), first of all well crushed powder of PVA and EYC were made by adding PVA and EYC in 10:1 ratio. The fine powder of PVA and EYC was well dispersed in 20ml of water and got milky white solution. The obtained milky solution was spread in the clean petriplate and put in the oven for 5h at 40°C. A transparent homogeneous EYCP film was obtained.

The structural morphology of the as synthesized samples was investigated using transmission electron microscopy (FEI Tecnai G2, USA). The crystalline structures of all Eu:Y2O3 based samples were characterized by X-Ray diffraction (‘X’ Pert Pro) using Cu-Kα1 radiation (λ =1.5405980Å). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of the samples were recorded using a PerkinElmer Spectrum 65, FT-IR spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by UV-VIS Spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer) with an excitation wavelength of 300nm. Raman measurements were performed on a micro-Raman setup (HR Lab Ram inverse system, Jobin Yvon Horiba). The 532nm line from a frequency doubled Nd:YAG laser (Coherent Compass) was used as excitation wavelength.

Y2O3 nanostructures doped with rare earth ions strongly depend on their synthesis methods and high temperature to support incorporation of rare earth ions in appropriate site which enhance fluorescence efficiency. Eu3+ ion doped Y2O3 complex (EYC) were synthesized employing auto combustion using normal homemade heater as shown in Fig. 1 . We anticipated that under high temperature in presence of urea, Eu3+ ion incorporate in Y2O3 lattice sites. As Y2O3 crystalline structure have Ia3 space group with cubic bixbyite structure containing 24 non-centrosymmetric C2 sites and 8 centrosymmetric C3i sites [27–29]. The C3i sites have center of inversion with small crystals which does not favor electron-dipole transitions in the absence of lattice distortion due to thermal or other impurity. However, the C2 sites have no center of inversion which allows electron-dipole transitions, and mostly responsible for the fluorescent emissions when incorporated by transition metal ions in C2 sites [30–32]. Eu3+ ions incorporate in both sites (C2 and C3i) of yttrium oxide but favoring sites are C2 even less concentration of Eu3+ ion, which results higher emission intensity from 5D0→7F2 transition [33,34]. However, incorporation of Eu3+ ions at the C3i site mostly contributes low emission intensity due to the lower probability of electronic transition from that C3i site [35,36].

Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) is a powerful tool, which can reveal the dimension, distribution and structural morphologies of the nanoparticles (NPs). The formation of nanostructures of Eu:Y2O3 NPs and Eu:Y2O3 NPs dispersed in PVA with different morphologies is confirmed by TEM. High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern analysis in both the cases, cubic flakes structure is found in agglomerated NPs. The crystallite size range is 20-60nm with average size of 35nm which also shows the good agreement with Scherrer's equation. Fig. 2(a)–(c) depicted the TEM image, HRTEM image and SAED of EYC NPs. From Fig. 2(a), it is found that the EYC NPs have the size between 20 and 60nm ranges. It shows the confirmation of NPs formation. Fig. 2(b) indicates the lattice fringes with interplanar spacing 3.08Å which corresponds to the distance of (222) plane in cubic EYC NPs. HRTEM suggests the high quality cubic crystal. The SAED pattern clearly indicates the crystalline nature of EYC NPs which are indexed as (211), (222), (400), (411), (440) and (622) planes. These planes suggest that Eu+3 ion is effectively caped into the Y2O3 host matrix [37]. Fig. 2(d)–(f) shows the TEM image and SAED pattern of EYC NPs dispersed in PVA film. From Fig. 2(d), it can be seen that EYC NPs are evenly distributed throughout the PVA film. Fig. 2(e) indicates the EYC NPs distribution in PVA. The average particles size of EYC in PVA is of the order of 35nm. Fig. 2(f) shows the SAED patterns of EYC NPs in PVA which indicates that crystalline nature of EYC NPs index as (211), (222), (400) and (440) plane. It shows that EYC NPs are well dispersed in PVA to make the EYCP film. The size of EYC NPs is between 20 and 60nm range. On comparing the particle size distribution in Eu:Y2O3 and Eu:Y2O3 with PVA, it is found that Eu:Y2O3 NPs are well dispersed as it is in the PVA. The average particles size in both cases is found of the order of 35nm [38].

The crystalline phase of the cubic phase of Eu3+ ion doped Y2O3 complex (EYC) was examined by the XRD pattern. XRD pattern of EYC is well matched with the standard JCPDS file of Y2O3 (JCPDS card no. 89-5591). The XRD pattern of EYC shows twelve distinct diffraction peaks corresponding to the reflection planes (211), (222), (400), (411), (332), (134), (125), (440), (611), (145), (622) and (136) respectively as shown in Fig. 3(a). The broadening of main diffraction peak at ∼28.77° is very less which is attributed to formation of nano-sized EYC according to Scherrer's formula. The XRD pattern of EYCP film shows a broad asymmetric diffraction peak at ∼25° (ranging from 18° to 29°). Moreover, the diffraction peak at ∼19.7° is well known peak associated with (101) reflection plane for PVA [39,40]. Here, the chain length and crystallinity of PVA may be changed due to attachment of PVA with EYC crystals resulting peak shifted to higher degree with asymmetric broad nature [39]. The particle size has been calculated by using Scherrer's formula:
d =
0.9
λ
β
cos θ
where d =size of the particle, λ =wavelength of the X-ray used (∼1.5414Å), β =FWHM of the peak, θ =diffraction angle.
The strongest four peaks (222) at 2θ =28.79, (400) at 2θ =33.39, (440) 2θ =48.19 and (622) and at 2θ =57.11 were used to calculate average crystallite size of the powder.
Putting all the values the average size of the particle is found to be ∼35nm, which is close to the TEM result.

The Raman spectra are presented in Fig. 3(b) with excitation wavelength of 532nm, which is clearly revealing the successful formation of EYC nanostructure and EYCP film. In the Raman spectrum of EYC, the F g and F g + E g vibrational mode of Eu3+ and Y2O3 were shown clearly at lower frequency region with high intensity [41]. Some intrinsic fluorescence bands were seen which can be due to impurity and transition of both. In fact, it is uneasy to distinguish the fluorescence peak from impurity or Eu3+/Y2O3. We anticipated that the spectrum of EYCP film is superposition of EYC and PVA and PVA does not affect the structure of EYC as Raman band of PVA is well known at ∼2900cm−1 [42].
The FTIR spectra of pure PVA film, EYC and EYCP film are examined in the range of 500–4000cm−1as shown in Fig. 4 (a).

In PVA film, a broad peak, centered at 3340cm−1 is examined. This is attributed to the symmetric stretching vibration of –OH from intramolecular to intermolecular hydrogen bond. Other bands are examined at 2920cm−1 and 2853cm−1, which indicates the symmetric and antisymmetric C–H stretching. The CO stretching peak is designated at 1651cm−1. Another band is found at 1423cm−1 which gives the information about CH2 bending vibration and CH2 wagging mode at 1328cm−1 . The C–C stretching and CH2 stretching are examined at 928cm−1 and 845cm−1 [43].

In prepared EYC sample, the small peaks are examined 1400cm−1 to 1700cm−1 which corresponds to the O–H bending of water molecules [38]. A peak at 1096cm−1 is designated C–O bending vibration due to impurity left during the synthesis process. One peak is appeared at 834cm−1 which is attributed to the Y–O stretching vibration [45]. The less intense broad bands at ∼3350cm−1 is well known and assigned to hydroxyl group stretching vibrations due to moisture.

In the addition of the rare earth EYC in PVA, we detect some structural modification. Some vibrations as PVA is appeared in the film as stated earlier as well as few new vibrational bands are also arises at 2137cm−1 and 2310cm−1 due to vibration of (CH+CC) and CO stretching. The two peaks at 1100cm−1 and 1389cm−1 corresponds to the C–O bending and C–O stretching. CC stretching is appeared at 1520cm−1 [46]. A peak is appeared at 834cm−1 which is due to the Y–O stretching vibration. These bands are confirming the formation of EYCP film as a composite.

Fig. 4(b) shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of EYC, PVA and EYCP film. PVA film exhibited a broad absorption characteristic peak at 278nm and a broad absorption of EYC has been found at 253nm [26]. This characteristic peak at 253nm has been originated due to the electronic excitation from the valence band O 2p electron to the conduction band Y 4d level. The absorption band for f-f transition is not examined due to very low concentration of Eu 3+ ions incorporated in the host site [43]. However, the absorption peak of EYCP is found at 246nm which is shifted towards the shorter wavelength region in comparison to EYC. This blue shift in the optical absorption edge is showcase of EYC particle attached with PVA matrix in nanometre regime and form a composite [44].

Fig. 5(a) shows excitation spectrum of EYC with 611nm detection wavelength. Excitation spectra consist one broad band at ∼257nm corresponding to O2−–Eu3+ charge transfer and several sharp peaks in large range region 283–450nm corresponds to 4f-4f transitions of Eu3+ ions [45]. Fig. 5(b) shows the emission spectra of EYC sample by direct excitation wavelength of 254nm. The emission spectra consist of several emission sharp peaks ranging from 560nm to 700nm corresponding to electrical dipole and magnetic dipole transitions from excited, 5D0→7Fj (j =0, 1, 2, 3, 4) energy levels of doped Eu3+ ion in Y2O3 host. The most sensitive transition 5D0→7F2 from energy levels of Eu3+ ion occupied C2 symmetry site of the Y2O3 host shows two peaks at ∼ 611nm with high intensity and low intensity at ∼630nm caused due to electrical dipole transition. On the other side, week emission peaks appear at ∼581nm corresponds to 5D0→7F0 transition, the emission at 587, 592 and 599nm correspond to the 5D0→7F1 transitions, the emission around at ∼649nm corresponds to 5D0→7F3 transition and the emission at ∼707nm corresponds to 5D0→7F4 transition. These several week emission peaks occurs mainly due to magnetic dipole transition, which is expected from the Judd–Ofelt selection rules (Δj =0, 1, 3 and 4) and independent on crystal structure [46,47]. Emission results preferring that the emission intensity depend on the Eu3+ ion occupied site of crystal which reveals the symmetry environment of incorporated Eu3+ ion [48].

The possible cross-relaxation transitions from Eu3+ to Y2O3 are indicated systematically in Fig. 6 . The energy level diagram of EYC is representing diverse paths for excitation and emission of incorporated Eu3+ ions in the Y2O3 host. Absorption 1 represents direct excitation from the lowest ground state (7F0) to the excited state of Eu3+ (5L5). Absorption 2 and 3 shows the indirect excitation from the ground states of Eu3+ to the excited states through Eu–O charge transfer with charge transfer energy ∼5eV and through the host Y2O3 to Eu3+ (5.8–6.2eV) respectively.

Further, the rich fluorescence of EYC provides a way for detection of hydrogen peroxide by quenching fluorescence of EYC. PL spectrum of EYC with different H2O2 concentrations, have been recorded as shown in Fig. 7(a) When, we increase concentration of H2O2 from 0 to 150μM fluorescence intensity decreases continuously without any peak shift. PL spectrum of EYCP film has been also recorded which clearly revels that PVA maintain luminescence properties of EYC. Therefore, fluorescent polymer film of EYC used as sensing chip for detection of H2O2, which is flexible and easy to handle. Fig. 7(b) shows selective H2O2 sensing via fluorescent EYCP film. When, we increase concentration of H2O2 from 0 to 150μM fluorescence intensity decreases continuously without any peak shift and when it reaches at 150μM, fluorescence fully quenched as shown in optical image of film Fig. 7(c). Fig. 7(c) shows photographs of EYCP film before excitation and after excitation with a UV lamp (254nm) in the presence of 0 and 150μM H2O2 respectively. It is clearly shown that H2O2 quenches the luminescence of EYCP film.

The concentration dependent quenching phenomenon is typically described by dynamic and static quenching. The dynamic quenching can be described by Stern–Volmer equation (1) and the static quenching can be expressed by the Lineweaver–Burk equation (2) as given below;
(1)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
s v
C q
(2)
1
(
F
0
− F
)
=
1
F
0
+
K LB
(
F
0
C q
)
where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of H2O2 respectively. C q is the concentration of H2O2, K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and K LB is the Lineweaver–Burk quenching constant. We have calculated K sv for EYC nanoparticles which were found to be 1.58×104M−1, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9965. Fig. 8(a) shows the variation of F 0/F as a function of C q and inset figure shows linear range over concentration 0–60μM of H2O2 for EYC NPs. For this ideal linear range, a plot of F 0/F versus C q will be linear with a slope equal to K sv and an intercept of approximate unity. Fig. 8(b) shows Lineweaver–Burk plot of static quenching mechanism for EYC NPs. Fig. 8(c) shows the variation of F 0/F as a function of C q and inset figure shows linear range over concentration 0–60μM of H2O2 for EYCP. For this ideal linear range, a plot of F 0/F versus C q will be linear with a slope equal to K sv and an intercept of approximate unity. K sv calculated 1.42×104 M−1, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9892. Fig. 8(d) shows Lineweaver–Burk plot of static quenching mechanism. Comparing, Fig. 8(a) and (b), the interaction between H2O2 and NPs has a dynamic mechanism. A linear relationship with H2O2 was observed using Stern–Volmer equation. Intensive quenching of PL intensity of PVA–EYC film by H2O2 exhibits a good linearity as shown in Fig. 8(d).

The value of K sv directly correlated with the sensitivity of the target molecules. Various reported K sv values for the determination of analytes using different nanostructures are listed in Table 1 . From the K sv value, H2O2 can be seen to have great fluorescence quenching efficiency by PVA–EYC film nearly EYC nanoparticles. The mechanism behind quenching can be assumed as follows: In this case hydrogen peroxide works as electron accepter which prevents the multi-phonon relaxation and radiative transition which results quenching in the fluorescence intensity as shown in Fig. 9 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for fluorescence quenching based H2O2 detection
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescence intensity arises from recombination of electron–hole pair which is well known mechanism, strongly depends on surface states. The quenching phenomena must follow some specific condition such as the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy of the fluorescence material must be higher than the energy level of electron accepting group, and electrostatic interaction occur between electron rich fluorescence material and analytes molecules [49]. The interaction of H2O2 molecule on the surface of fluorescence film reduces recombination of electron–hole pair.

Charge transfer and quenching in fluorescence is shown in Fig. 9. The excitation band of EYC is attributed to charge transfer from the 2p orbital of oxygen to the 4f orbital of europium. The highest intensity fluorescence signal at 611nm originates from 5D0→7F2 transition for Eu3+ and other D–F transitions of Eu3+ are too week as explained above. The PL quenching mechanism can be occurs due to charge transfer from the Eu-O excited transfer state to H2O2 molecule rather than 5D0 state of Eu3+, which reduce the number of electron in 5D0 state. So, the reduction in charge transfer from emission from 5D0→7Fjthe emission from Eu centers decreases.

It is found that quenching of emission of 5D0→7F2 are almost same in EYC and EYCP. The PL intensity in the EYCP film is slightly decreases as compared to EYC. The emission spectra show that 5D0→7F2 transition of Eu3+ in the PVA composite (EYCP) red shift in contrast to the pure Eu:Y2O3 (EYC) complex. This is due to relative contribution of different stark component of 5D0→7F2 transition. Our result shows that by the incorporation of Eu complex in PVA, the Eu3+ ions exhibits different local environments because of influence of surrounding PVA. Hence symmetry of the coordination sphere of Eu3+decreases slightly in EYCP film. So the emission spectra shifted slightly towards red in PVA composite [50,51].

We can also explain PL quenching as the oxidized form of EYC is colorless, but the reduced form of EYC in film is pink in presence of UV light which turns colorless in addition of H2O2 because the reduced species of EYC can react with H2O2 to the oxidized EYC according to Eq. (3) [52]. Therefore it is irreversible in nature.
(3)
EYC
(
Eu
3 +
) +
H 2
O 2
⟶
UV
Light
EYC
( oxidized ) +
H 2
O

Now we have performed the H2O2 sensing with Eu:Y2O3 complex. We have found that in complex the emission peak of 5D0→7F2 are quenched similarly as it in the PVA film. On increasing the concentration of H2O2 the emission of Eu3+ decreases and completely quenched at 150μM as shown in Fig. 7.
A comparative study of different constants calculated in EYC and EYCP film are given in Table 1 and compared with some reported analytes as given below.

In summary, we have prepared highly dispersed Eu:Y2O3 fluorescent flexible film of poly vinyl alcohol (EYCP film) for non-enzymatic detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) by turn off probe of fluorescence. Eu:Y2O3 have been synthesized by one step heat combustion method and dispersed on poly vinyl alcohol using simple mixing methods to make EYCP fluorescent film. The fluorescence spectra of EYCP consisting number of emission sharp peaks ranging from 560nm to 700nm corresponding to electrical dipole and magnetic dipole transitions from excited 5D0→7Fj (j =0, 1, 2, 3, 4) energy levels of doped Eu3+ ion in Y2O3 host. These excitation energy bands attributed to charge transfer from the 2p orbital of oxygen to the 4f orbital of europium. The intense peak occurs at ∼611nm corresponding to 5D0–7F2 transition. In the presence of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of the EYCP film was quenched due to the reduction of electron–hole pair, recombination in Eu center. The EYCP film shows excellent fluorescence quenching in presence of H2O2 by significantly increasing concentration of H2O2 and completely quenched at ∼150μM. A comparative study fluorescence quenching of the EYCP film is also done with EYC nanoparticles and found the nearly same result in both the cases. This work gives insight for synthesis of rare earth metal doped oxide flexible film with great fluorescence properties and makes a room for further development of high quality film for efficient biosensing applications.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors (SKS) is thankful to UGC, New Delhi Project No. (P-01/672) for providing financial assistance. One of the authors, AS is thankful to DST, New Delhi project no. (DST/TSG/PT/2012/68) for providing the financial support. MS is thankful to DST New Delhi for providing the financial assistance.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dhananjay Kumar received his M.Sc. degree in 2011 from Banaras Hindu University. He is currently a Ph.D. student of Banaras Hindu University. His research interest is in novel study of optoelectronic materials and devices.

Sima Umrao received her B.Sc. degree in science and M.Sc. degree in physics from the University of Kanpur, Kanpur, India, in 2005 and 2007, respectively. In 2015, she received Ph.D. degrees in physics from the Institute of Science, Department of Physics, B.H.U., India. In 2016, she awarded by Brain Korea 21 Plus (BK 21+) postdoctoral fellowship from the South Korea government. She joined the SKKU Advanced Institute of Nanotechnology (SAINT), Sungkyunkwan University, South Korea, as a postdoctoral researcher in February 2016. Her current research interests include 2D materials based nano-electronic devices, metal oxide and their hybrids with 2D materials for photocatalyst, and biosensing.

Himanshu Mishra received his M.Sc. degree from the Department of Physics, Banaras Hindu University, India in year 2011. Currently, he is pursuing Ph.D. from the Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, India. His research interest lies in 2D materials (graphene and beyond) for electronic, optoelectronic and energy harvesting devices, fluorescent nanostructures and their. biological applications

Rohit Ranjan Srivastava received his M.Sc. from D.D.U. Gorakhpur University in 2013. He is pursuing Ph.D. from Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University. Presently, he is working on synthesis and characterization of novel 2D materials.

Dr. Monika Srivastava is a Post-Doctoral Fellow, working in the School of Materials Science and Technology, IIT, BHU. She has received her Ph.D. from Department of Chemistry, Banaras Hindu University in 2003. She is having expertise in non-conventional electrochemical techniques for synthesis of polymers and composites. She is currently working towards synthesis and application of nano composites for sensors and drugs analysis. She has published a number of research papers in this area.

Anchal Srivastava received his Ph.D. from Banaras Hindu University in 2002. He continued in the same department as a Post-Doctoral Fellow and later joined the department as Lecturer in 2004. He is currently serving as an Associate Professor at Department of Physics, Institute of Science, B.H.U. He has expertise in the growth and application of carbon based nanomaterials. He has been awarded the Max Planck India fellowship and carried out his research at Max Planck Institute of Solid State Physics, Stuttgart, Germany with the group of Professor Klaus Von Klitzing from 2008 to 2011. He has also been awarded the prestigious BOYSCAST fellowship from government of India in 2008–09 during which he carried out research on graphene and related materials with Professor P.M. Ajayan at Rice University. He has also been a Visiting Research Scholar at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, USA in 2006.

S.K. Srivastava did his M.Sc. from Banaras Hindu University Varanasi in year 1998. He received his Ph.D. from IIT (BHU) Varanasi in 2002. He continued in the same department as a Post-Doctoral Fellow of CSIR New Delhi for one year in IIT (BHU) Varanasi and later joined as a Lecturer in 2003 in Government Engineering College (MITS) Gwalior. After that he joined BHU Varanasi as an Assistant Professor in Department of Physics (MMV), BHU, Varanasi. He is currently serving as an Associate Professor Department of Physics (MMV), B.H.U. Varanasi. He specializes in the area of optical waveguide, Photonic crystal and Application, Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensor, synthesis, characterizations and applications of nanomaterials and graphene. He has been awarded the CSIR research fellowship and UGC research fellowship. He is also active member of different administrative committee of BHU Varanasi. He has also the many research projects of UGC and AICTE New Delhi. He has also supervised the many M.Sc. Projects and Ph.D. thesis. He has also published more than 30 research papers in the different journal of international repute.
